name: RMS Rhone
validation_source: Blue Water Divers, DiveBVI.com, PADI dive site guides

tags.notes: Maximum depth 26m (85ft). Bow at 24m, stern at 9m. Very open wreck with few entrapment hazards. World-class Caribbean wreck dive.

name: The Indians
validation_source: ScubaBoard BVI diving discussions, dive operator sites

tags.notes: Four rocky pinnacles. Shallow side 3m coral garden, deeper side steep wall to 11m. Excellent marine life including snapper, dolphins, turtles. Can have strong currents.

name: The Caves at Norman Island
validation_source: BVI-Dive-Map.com, epic yacht charters, local dive guides

tags.notes: Three water-level caves at Norman Island. First cave extends ~27m. Popular for snorkeling and shallow diving. Great for beginners and snorkelers.
Deepwater pinnacle 1.5km SE of Norman Island. Depths 6-31m. Craggy top with fire coral. Distinctive 'sink hole' feature. Pelagic fish and sharks. Watch for heavy fire coral.

name: Blonde Rock
validation_source: BVIDiving.com, Sail Caribbean Divers, dive operator sites

tags.notes: Shallow pinnacle between Salt Island and Dead Chest Island. Rises from 18m to 5m. Complex system of tunnels, overhangs, undercuts. Abundant lobsters. Weather-dependent.
Off Ginger Island south shore. Spur and groove reef 11-21m. Outstanding visibility. Mushroom-shaped coral formations. Eagle rays, stingrays, turtles common. Easy navigation.

name: Wreck Alley
validation_source: PADI Dive Site guides, ZuBlu, BVIDiving.com, Reef Smart Guides

tags.notes: Four scuttled wrecks (Pat, Beata, Mary L, Island Seal) off Cooper Island. Depths ~27m. Garden eels, rays, snappers, groupers, moray eels. Thriving artificial reef.

name: Anegada Reef
validation_source: PADI dive guides, Dive and Cruise, DiveScore

tags.notes: Part of 29km Horseshoe Reef - largest barrier reef in Caribbean. Pristine reefs, nurse sharks, rays, turtles. Access limited to those with local guide due to shallow and coral-dense conditions.

name: The Chimney at Great Dog Island

tags.notes: Two rocky formations create a chimney gap. Protected northwest of Great Dog Island. Depths 9-12m. Shrimps, damselfish, parrotfish, scorpionfish. Famous Jacques Cousteau site.
East of Cooper Island. Ridge rises from 24m. Open water site with pillar corals, black corals, sea fans. Pelagic fish (cobia, African pompano). Can have strong currents - heed dive briefing.

Three dramatic step walls on Ginger Island southwest: 12m, 20m, 29m. Tube sponges, sea fans, corals. Protected from currents. White sand reflects sunlight creating magical scenery. Suitable for all levels.

name: Playgrounds Anegada
validation_source: PADI dive guides, Anegada diving information

tags.notes: Shallow reef site around Anegada. Part of pristine Horseshoe Reef system. Excellent for beginner divers. Requires local guide access due to shallow waters and coral maze characteristics.
Southwest of Norman Island. Captivating canyons and ridges with gorgonians. Marine life includes jacks, drums, blennies, angelfish, seahorses, jawfish, turtles, rays, and nurse sharks.
Northwestern tip of George Dog, allegedly favored by Jacques Cousteau. Features ridges, archways, canyons with soft and pillar coral. Limited visibility; flashlights recommended.
Between Norman Island and Peter Island. Two pinnacles in open water. One of the most exciting night dive sites in the BVI. Named after neighboring Carrot Rock.
West side of Dead Chest Island. Multi-level formations with overhangs, caves, ridges, passages, and archways. Snappers, fairy basslets, glassy sweepers, green moray eels, lobsters.
Northern side of Ginger Island in Sir Francis Drake Channel. Crabs, lobsters, octopuses, turtles, barracuda, nurse and reef sharks, tarpons, moray eels. Good for macro photography.
Hidden gem with a fiery red wall and rocky formations covered with corals and sponges. Parrotfish, grunts, turtles, lobsters, tarpons. Experiences big swells.
West of West Dog Island. Underwater boulders resembling Bedrock. Rocky formations with ledges and crevices. Snappers, gold-spotted eels, cobias, lobsters, eagle rays, nurse sharks.
South of Cooper Island. Relatively uncrowded. Black durgons, snappers, jacks, groupers, mackerels, pompanos, spadefish, barracuda, lobsters. Good for underwater photography.
Near Dead Chest Island. Most vivid, picturesque dive site in the BVI. Four gullies with canyons, walls, and a narrow cave. Bold yellow, red, orange, purple corals. Hawksbill turtles, nurse sharks.

Near Mountain Point and Cow's Mouth. Stunning underwater caves and hills covered in corals and gorgonians. Fire coral, staghorn, elkhorn, cup coral. Arrow crabs, fairy basslets, lobsters, lionfish, turtles.

Southwestern BVI, 12 minutes from Spanish Town. Massive pinnacle with caves, canyons, ledges. Glassy sweepers, tarpons, permits, barracudas, spadefish, turtles, eagle rays, reef sharks. Best in summer.
Uninhabited islet between Tortola and Virgin Gorda, part of Dog Islands group. National park since 1974. Caves, ledges, rocky formations covered with colorful sponges and corals. Secluded.
Northeast of Dead Chest Island. Brain, sheet, and star corals, large sea fans. Puffer fish, filefish, chub, snappers, goatfish, damselfish, squirrelfish, jawfish, barracuda.
Eastern side of Brewers Bay, northern Tortola. Isolated rocky formations on sandy bottom. Glassy sweepers, tarpons, southern stingrays, nurse sharks. Quieter alternative to popular sites.

Two sunken ships. The Fearless is believed to be sister ship of Cousteau's Calypso. The Willy T was a floating bar/restaurant. Both wooden wrecks severely degraded - caution required. Fairy basslets, eagle rays, turtles.
136-foot island freighter deliberately sunk in 1996 by BVI Dive Operators Association. Rests upright on sand. Divers can investigate cargo hold and corridors. Lizardfish, snappers, grunts, stingrays.

Between The Baths and Spanish Town, Virgin Gorda. Rocky formations, small caves, granite boulders from volcanic activity. Chromis, angelfish, parrotfish, moorish idols, moray eels, lobsters, stingrays, nurse sharks.
Next to Joe's Cave on West Dog, southwest tip closest to Scrub Island. Novice-level wall dive with low swell and low current.
Small islet northwest of Virgin Gorda in The Dogs group. Rocky formations with ledges and crevices. Open waters with strong currents. Colorful exotic fish. Good for underwater photography.

1,500-foot-long wall on north side of Norman Island. Named after Spyglass Hill from Treasure Island. Sea fans, purple tube sponges. Fairy basslets, damselfish, tarpons, eagle rays, stingrays, turtles.

Between Cooper and Ginger Islands. Magnificent rocky formation with boulders densely populated by fish. Sharks, barracuda, moray eels, lobsters, amberjacks, groupers, spotted drums. Not heavily frequented.

Northwest side of Virgin Gorda near Mountain Point. Named after rock shaped like a cow's mouth. Great visibility up to 100 feet. Barracuda, eagle rays, jacks. Experienced divers can explore inside the rock formation.

Six miles west of Jost Van Dyke. National Park with third largest seabird population in Caribbean. Over 15 diving sites around the islands. Mercurious Rock cave system. Strict no-anchor policy. Black corals.

North of Virgin Gorda. Submerged pinnacle rising to 4 feet from surface with no marker buoy. Colorful coral and sponges. Strong currents bring many fish. Only suitable for less experienced divers in calm weather.

Near Wreck Alley. Massive underwater pinnacle resembling a thumb. Tarpons, French angelfish, queen angelfish, spotted drums, seahorses, lobsters, barracuda. Superior marine biodiversity. Excellent for photography.

South of Guana Island with white sand beach. Aquarium-like waters with nice walls and smaller rocky formations. Grunts, yellowtail snappers, yellow jacks, turtles. Fish reportedly allow close approach.
Near Scrub Island. System of ledges at ~18m full of sea life. Monitor open water for rays and sharks. Small caves and canyons. Higher currents typical. Head east from mooring to access ledge system.

Near the RMS Rhone wreck. Magnificent colored reef with soft and hard corals. Colorful exotic fish, turtles, nurse sharks, occasional reef sharks. Rocky canyons and small crevices. Weather-independent but can have currents.

Less crowded site ideal for looping around schools of fish. Giant barrel sponges, golden zoanthids, crinoids, gorgonians. Butterflyfish, rock beauties, bar jacks, yellowhead jawfish, moray eels, lobsters.
Northern side of George Dog. Eastern canyons, western ledges. Smaller pelagic fish including barracuda, bonitos, mackerels, tarpons. Weather-dependent; best in summer. Often overlooked.

Uninhabited National Park between Salt and Peter Island. Three diving zones: Coral Gardens, Dead Chest Wall, and Painted Walls. Named after pirate legend of Edward Teach (Blackbeard). Fairy basslets, grunts, snappers.

Eastern side of Salt Island near Cooper Island. 'Sergeant Major City' and 'Sergeant Major Suburbs' pillar coral formations. Anemones, shrimps, squirrelfish, jawfish, moray eels, lobsters, nurse sharks. Near RMS Rhone.
South of Norman Island. Maze of ledges, canyons, and caverns with an open cave. Queen angelfish, black durgon, barracuda, stingrays, lobsters, eels, sea turtles. Labyrinth of canyons and tunnels.

North of Norman Island near Pelican Island. Juvenile trunkfish, spotted drums, creole wrasse, garden eels, queen angelfish, nurse sharks. Abundant corals, sponges, gorgonians. Incredible photographic potential.
East of Little Harbour. Deep water harbor with moorings. Large rays in front of beach club. Caution with local fishermen's nets in afternoon.

Between Fearless and Randy's Reef. Features the anchor from the RMS Rhone, lost on October 29, 1867 when the captain attempted to raise it during bad weather. Anchor covered in colorful coral growth. Blennies, wrasses, damselfish.

Between Tortola and Anegada. 246-foot refrigerated cargo vessel sunk 1981. Adorned with colorful corals and sponges. Horse-eyed jacks, jewfish, barracuda, cobia, groupers, stingrays, turtles, and a resident 600-pound Goliath grouper.

Western side of Cooper Island. Craters, ledges, rocky outcroppings with sea fans. Blue tangs, queen angelfish, snappers, grunts, tarpon, turtles, stingrays, moray eels, nurse sharks. Quieter than nearby sites.
Uninhabited island south of Virgin Gorda. National Park since 1974, bird sanctuary since 1959. Heavy volcanic boulders. Underwater caves, tunnels, and rocky formations.
Named after the blue chromis fish. Sea fans, gorgonians, quillfin blennies on sandy seafloor. Depth range 9-27m. Straightforward layout, difficult to get lost. Good for training dives.

Beautiful coral formations combined with an airplane wreck placed as part of BVI reef program. Aircraft lacks wings and tail but has become center of sea life. Lobsters, stingrays, barracuda, occasional turtles.
Hidden gem with uncrowded white sand beaches. Amazing offshore coral reef with colorful tropical fish. Great visibility due to shallow depths. Shore entry via dirt road.
Off Great Camanoe, 2 minutes from Scrub Island. Soft and hard corals with colorful sponges. Sergeant majors, grey snappers, blue chromis, wrasses, parrotfish, garden eels, stingrays, turtles.
Western side of Brewers Bay, northern Tortola. Isolated rocky pinnacles rising to different heights. Baitfish, blennies, mackerel, tarpon, lobsters. Secluded site without crowds.
East of Green Cay West near Little Jost Van Dyke. Corals and gorgonians with exciting pinnacles on north side. Angelfish, parrotfish, dog snappers, reef squids, manta rays, reef sharks, tarpons.

Northwest of Virgin Gorda. Sandy bottom with corals and sea sponges. Caves, passages, rock formations with sleeping nurse sharks. Northern reef in excellent condition. Grunts, parrotfish, angelfish, tarpons, jacks, stingrays.

West of West Dog Island. Huge boulders up to 25 feet high on sandy bottom. Underwater cave narrow at top, widens at base. Giant groupers, lobsters, eagle rays, glassy sweepers. Colorful corals and sponges on cave walls.
